self_report.reflection: The integrand t^{-1/2} e^{-a(t+1/t)} is sharply peaked at t=1, where t+1/t=2, so the exponential factor must be e^{-2a}, not e^{-a}. Substituting t=u^2 gives 2e^{-2a}∫_0^∞ e^{-a(u-1/u)^2}du; the involution u↦1/u together with dv=(1+1/u^2)du collapses this to the Gaussian ∫_{-∞}^∞ e^{-av^2}dv = √(π/a), so I(a)=√(π/a) e^{-2a}. The asserted e^{-a} is wrong by a factor of e^{a}.
self_report.notes: Standard Putnam 1985 B5 closed form is √(π/a) e^{-2a}; the asserted exponent is off by a factor of e^{a}.
